content.text: Managing Files in The Files API

encompasses the complete lifecycle of files after upload: listing stored files, retrieving metadata, downloading content, and deleting files to prevent storage accumulation—all critical for maintaining efficient document workflows and avoiding unnecessary costs.
that allow you to view, inspect, retrieve, and clean up files stored in Claude's cloud, including pagination, metadata access, and deletion workflows.

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

# List all files in your workspace
files = client.beta.files.list()

for file in files:
    print(f"{file.id} - {file.filename} ({file.size_bytes} bytes)")

file_abc123xyz - contract.pdf (1048576 bytes)
file_xyz789abc - report.txt (524288 bytes)
file_def456ghi - photo.png (2097152 bytes)

# Paginate through many files
files = client.beta.files.list(limit=100)

for file in files.data:
    print(f"{file.id}: {file.filename}")

# Get next page
next_page = client.beta.files.list(after=files.last_id, limit=100)

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

# Get metadata for a specific file
file_info = client.beta.files.retrieve(file_id="file_abc123xyz")

print(f"Filename: {file_info.filename}")
print(f"Size: {file_info.size_bytes} bytes")
print(f"MIME Type: {file_info.mime_type}")
print(f"Created: {file_info.created_at}")
print(f"Downloadable: {file_info.downloadable}")

Filename: financial_report.pdf
Size: 1048576 bytes
MIME Type: application/pdf
Created: 2024-09-23T10:30:00Z
Downloadable: true

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

# Download file content
file_content = client.beta.files.download(file_id="file_abc123xyz")

# Save to local file
with open("downloaded_report.pdf", "wb") as f:
    f.write(file_content)

print("File downloaded successfully")
"Download restrictions: can only download files the API generates, not files you uploaded."

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

# Upload file
uploaded = client.beta.files.upload(
    file=(
        "temp_report.pdf",
        open("temp_report.pdf", "rb"),
        "application/pdf"
    )
)
file_id = uploaded.id

# Use the file...
# ... (do work)

# Delete when done
client.beta.files.delete(file_id)
print(f"File deleted: {file_id}")

- Files persist indefinitely
- Storage costs accumulate
- 500 GB org quota consumed
- File IDs remain discoverable

- Files removed from storage
- No further costs
- File ID becomes invalid
- Cannot reference in future requests

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

# STEP 1: List existing files (audit)
print("=== Current Files ===")
files = client.beta.files.list()
for file in files:
    print(f"{file.id} - {file.filename} ({file.size_bytes} bytes)")

# STEP 2: Upload new file
print("\n=== Uploading ===")
uploaded = client.beta.files.upload(
    file=(
        "contract.pdf",
        open("contract.pdf", "rb"),
        "application/pdf"
    )
)
file_id = uploaded.id
print(f"✓ Uploaded: {file_id}")

# STEP 3: Get metadata
print("\n=== File Metadata ===")
file_info = client.beta.files.retrieve(file_id=file_id)
print(f"Filename: {file_info.filename}")
print(f"Size: {file_info.size_bytes} bytes")
print(f"MIME Type: {file_info.mime_type}")
print(f"Created: {file_info.created_at}")

# STEP 4: Use file in Messages
print("\n=== Using File ===")
response = client.beta.messages.create(
    model="claude-opus-4-8",
    max_tokens=16000,
    messages=[{
        "role": "user",
        "content": [
            {"type": "text", "text": "What are the key terms?"},
            {
                "type": "document",
                "source": {"type": "file", "file_id": file_id}
            }
        ]
    }]
)
print(f"Answer: {response.content[0].text[:200]}...")

# STEP 5: Delete when done
print("\n=== Cleaning Up ===")
client.beta.files.delete(file_id)
print(f"✓ Deleted: {file_id}")

# STEP 6: Verify deletion (confirm)
print("\n=== Files After Deletion ===")
files = client.beta.files.list()
print(f"Total files remaining: {len(files)}")
Optional expiration policy (not set by default)

- Storage costs accumulate if files not deleted
- 500 GB quota consumed per organization

# Clean up old files
files = client.beta.files.list()

# Find files older than 30 days
import datetime
cutoff = datetime.datetime.now() - datetime.timedelta(days=30)

for file in files:
    created = datetime.datetime.fromisoformat(file.created_at)
    if created < cutoff:
        client.beta.files.delete(file.id)
        print(f"Deleted: {file.filename}")
List files to find old ones
Store IDs in database for reuse
Files tied to workspace, not portable
When working with managing files, following established best practices ensures reliability, maintainability, and optimal performance.
Start simple and iterate — begin with the minimum viable implementation before adding complexity.
Test with representative inputs — ensure your test cases cover edge cases, not just the happy path.
Monitor performance — track latency, token usage, and error rates in production environments.
Version your prompts and configurations — treat prompt text like source code with proper version control.
Document your design decisions — record why you chose specific approaches for future maintainers.
Plan for failure — implement graceful degradation when the AI component is unavailable or returns unexpected results.
Review outputs regularly — periodically audit what the system produces to catch quality drift over time.
These pitfalls frequently trip up developers working with managing files. Knowing them in advance saves significant debugging time.
Assuming deterministic output — language models are probabilistic; the same input can produce different outputs across calls. Always validate the structure of responses.
Ignoring token limits — exceeding context window limits causes truncation or errors. Track token counts, especially in multi-turn conversations.
Over-engineering early — building complex infrastructure before validating basic functionality wastes effort. Get a working prototype first.
Hardcoding prompts in code — embed prompts in configuration files or databases so non-developers can iterate on them.
Skipping error handling — API calls fail due to rate limits, timeouts, or content policy violations. Always wrap calls in try/catch with appropriate retry logic.
As your usage of managing files scales, these performance factors become increasingly important.
Latency budget — typical API latency ranges from 1-30 seconds depending on model size and output length. Design your UX around streaming to provide early feedback.
Concurrency limits — each API tier has request-per-minute (RPM) and token-per-minute (TPM) limits. Implement request queuing for burst traffic.
Prompt caching — use the cache_control breakpoint on stable, repeated prompt prefixes to reduce costs by up to 90% and latency by 85%.
Batching — for non-interactive workloads, the Message Batches API processes up to 10,000 requests asynchronously at 50% cost reduction.
Model selection — use Claude Haiku for high-volume, low-complexity tasks and Claude Sonnet or Opus for complex reasoning where quality matters more than speed.
